prompt:
Security review just killed the eval() call in our alert-rule engine (rightly — customers type these formulas into a text box) and now I owe them a real evaluator. Module exprcalc.py, one entry point `evaluate(source, variables=None)`, plus an `ExprError` exception class. eval/exec/compile/ast are all off the table, tokenize and parse it yourself.

The language: integer and decimal literals (no scientific notation), variable names ([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*) looked up in the variables dict, parens, and operators with sane precedence — from loosest to tightest: `or`, `and`, `not`, comparisons (< <= > >= == !=), + -, * / %, unary minus, ** (right-associative, and unary minus binds LOOSER than ** so -2**2 is -4). Comparisons do NOT chain — `1 < 2 < 3` is a syntax error, our users always mean something else when they write that. `and`/`or`/`not` work on truthiness (0 and 0.0 are false) but always RETURN a plain bool, never the operand. Comparisons return bools. Ints stay ints through + - * % **, `/` is always float division. Watch the lexer: `nothing` and `android` are variable names, not `not`/`and` plus leftovers.

Three built-in functions: min and max (one or more args), abs (exactly one). A name is a function call only when it's immediately followed by `(` — otherwise it's just a variable, even if it's spelled min.

Errors are half the feature, our support team reads these all day. Every failure raises ExprError carrying a `.pos` attribute — 0-based character offset into the source: the offending character for garbage input, the token that wasn't allowed there, the name for unknown variables/functions and wrong argument counts (and put the name in the message), the operator for division-or-modulo by zero, and len(source) when the input just stops too early. 

test_exprcalc.py is the acceptance contract; python3 test_exprcalc.py must print ok and you don't get to edit it. Stdlib only.
